channel.title: Just In
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026, Australian Broadcasting Corporation. All Rights Reserved.

item.title: Regional housing crisis '25 years in the making'
item.description: Australia's regional housing crisis is decades in the making, the head of a research institute says. So can the federal budget fix it?

media:group.media:description: With so many houses being used as short stay, Lara Howell says policy change can't come soon enough.

item.title: Why Aung San Suu Kyi's son did not celebrate her move to house arrest
item.description: Aung San Suu Kyi's son demands proof his mother is still alive, as Myanmar's military junta keeps tens of thousands of political prisoners behind bars.
item.dc:creator: Libby Hogan

media:group.media:description: Aung San Suu Kyi has not had access to her family or lawyer since being imprisoned by the military.

item.title: Deaf and blind cricketers angered by changes to Cricket Australia's funding
item.description: Significant changes to how Cricket Australia funds Deaf, blind and intellectually disabled teams prompt concern from players and advocates.
item.dc:creator: Bridget McArthur

media:group.media:description: Callum Asbury says Cricket Australia's announcement has left many players feeling angry.

item.title: Mother's Day gratitude from a former foster child
item.description: Jaharn grew up in foster care from two years of age. Now 19, he and his foster mum share their lessons of love.
item.dc:creator: Kris Flanders

media:group.media:description: Fun times and lots of smiles. Selina and Jaharn's bond is strong.

item.title: Some nannies working without superannuation due to 'discriminatory' law
item.description: Despite working full-time hours, some private domestic workers are going years without superannuation contributions. Now, industry stakeholders are calling for change.
item.dc:creator: Adelaide Miller

media:group.media:description: Jess Renn says changing the super laws would mean nannies are protected.

item.title: Australians with motor neurone disease fear losing fast-tracked NDIS
item.description: Australians with MND who have rapidly changing needs fear they will lose a priority pathway to access the National Disability Insurance Scheme under Labor’s planned changes.
item.dc:creator: Clare Armstrong

media:group.media:description: Todd Johnson was diagnosed with MND four years ago.

item.title: Coalition's EV strategy risks appearing out of touch
item.description: The climate war is being won quietly, with nearly three in four One Nation voters eager to buy an EV.
item.dc:creator: Jacob Greber

media:group.media:description: According to the Nationals leader Matt Canavan the policy is a "completely failed scheme".

item.title: Keeping 'ISIS brides' out of sight will not make Australia safer
item.description: The political energy spent on the "ISIS brides" has not been matched by serious attention to the deficiencies in Australia's counterterrorism architecture.
item.dc:creator: Sean Rubinsztein-Dunlop

media:group.media:description: In his response to the women and children who have been desperate to return to Australia, the prime minister has been fond of quoting his mother's advice: "If you make your bed, you lie in it."

item.title: Voters in Farrer set to elect new MP after three months in spotlight
item.description: After three months of intense campaigning, voters in the NSW electorate say whoever wins today's poll must act on the promise to make noise in Canberra.
item.dc:creator: Melinda Hayter

media:group.media:description: Paul Pierotti warns the incoming member for Farrer that voters will demand action on issues raised ahead of the by-election.

item.title: Military parades and promises of peace ring hollower than ever
item.description: Displays of military strength and perpetual promises of peace do little to mask the reality that major powers aren't always able to control the wars they helped create.
item.dc:creator: Laura Tingle

media:group.media:description: Despite Trump's disdain for his allies, the significant damage to US bases and military assets in the Middle East that are increasingly being reported means his military's physical options now seem more constrained.

item.title: Biofuels could ease our energy woes. But the industry says it needs a mandate
item.description: Billions of dollars worth of investment will flow if the federal government mandates a percentage of biofuels be added to the nation's fuel supply, major investors say.
item.dc:creator: Daniel Ziffer

media:group.media:description: Canola, like this crop in Naracoorte, is largely exported to make low carbon fuels overseas.

item.title: Inside India's $130 billion wedding obsession
item.description: Foreign Correspondent crosses India to see how marriage has become big business.
item.dc:creator: Meghna Bali and Marty Smiley

media:group.media:description: Shikhar Mehta and Khushi Parmar dance at their wedding.

item.title: Some people will hate Tuesday's federal budget but Jim Chalmers doesn't mind
item.description: For once, the budget won't be a dull one. Jim Chalmers's tax reforms will create a stir and some question whether they will close the generation gap, but the treasurer is projecting a relaxed figure.
item.dc:creator: Tom Crowley

media:group.media:description: Jim Chalmers is preparing his fifth and most consequential federal budget.

item.title: ABC 50/50 quiz: Test your knowledge of a Hitchcock classic, a popular dog breed and Gotye vs Carly Rae
item.description: Each question in the ABC 50/50 quiz has only two options: one is right, and the other is wrong. But there's a catch.

media:group.media:description: Cary Grant as Roger Thornhill evades a crop dusting plane in a cornfield in a famous scene from Alfred Hitchcock's North by Northwest.

item.title: The day Australia's 'legislative machinery' was set in motion
item.description: More than 12,000 people packed into Melbourne's Royal Exhibition Building to witness the "pomp and ceremony" of a "magnificent" occasion in the country's history.
item.dc:creator: Tim Callanan

media:group.media:description: The faces of more than 340 dignitaries are identifiable in Nuttall's painting.

item.title: How a case of 'microplastic brain worm' led to this author's debut novel
item.description: Ellena Savage's debut essay collection, Blueberries, was a surprise hit. In her new novel, The Ruiners, she takes on Charles Dickens and the illegal waste trade.
item.dc:creator: Nicola Heath

media:group.media:description: "I didn't know that writing about myself so intimately was a transgressive thing," Savage says of the reaction to her debut essay collection, Blueberries.

item.title: UK PM 'not going to walk away' despite catastrophic local election defeats
item.description: Sir Keir Starmer's Labour party has lost hundreds of seats in the local government election in England and has admitted defeat in Wales - which was previously Labour heartland.

media:group.media:description: Sir Keir Starmer has vowed to stay on as UK Prime Minister.

item.title: Hostages freed after German bank robbery, suspects on run
item.description: Two hostages have been freed unhurt after being held in a bank in the German town of Sinzig, but despite heavily-armed police surrounding the bank, the suspects were able to escape.

media:group.media:description: Two captives were locked inside the bank's vault but were freed by police unharmed.

item.title: Migration, crypto and red tape to feature in budget bid for economic growth
item.description: Treasurer Jim Chalmers says Australia's productivity problem will take a long time to fix, but a $10 billion annual saving on business compliance costs is a start.
item.dc:creator: Tom Crowley

media:group.media:description: Jim Chalmers says the government needs to act "on a broad range of fronts" to fix Australia's productivity problem.

item.title: Japan confirms first fatal bear attack this year with two more suspected
item.description: Japanese authorities say a 55-year-old woman found dead last month was killed by a bear. It is the first confirmed fatal bear attack for 2026 following a record 13 deaths last year.

media:group.media:description: A boom in bear numbers in Japan has led to increasing encounters.

item.title: Probe makes adverse findings against former ANU Council members
item.description: An independent review into 36 allegations concerning the Australian National University has made a series of adverse findings against former council members.
item.dc:creator: Monte Bovill

media:group.media:description: An independent review into 36 allegations concerning the ANU has found a series of adverse findings.

item.title: 'I don't coach for my job': Voss defiant after latest loss, Dogs edge Port
item.description: Carlton coach Michael Voss is frustrated after a close loss to Brisbane, but denies he is coaching for his job, while the Western Bulldogs hold off Port Adelaide despite being held to one goal in the second half.
item.dc:creator: Chris De Silva and Andrew McGarry

media:group.media:description: Michael Voss has said his team cannot become comfortable with mediocrity after a close loss to Brisbane.

item.title: Government backbencher criticises 'giant, bloated, ineffective' NT public service
item.description: A Northern Territory government backbencher has criticised the "giant, bloated and ineffective" NT public service, saying its current size is not feasible.
item.dc:creator: Jack Hislop

media:group.media:description: Tanzil Rahman says the NT public service is too large.

item.title: SA premier 'very concerned' over Whyalla steelworks' blast furnace
item.description: Whyalla steelworkers are struggling to get the city's blast furnace producing iron again, with SA Premier Peter Malinauskas saying he is "very concerned" about the situation.
item.dc:creator: James Wakelin

media:group.media:description: The Whyalla steelworks went into administration more than a year ago.

item.title: Man who drunkenly performed Nazi salute in front of cops fined $1,400
item.description: The man pleaded guilty to four charges, with a magistrate describing the Nazi gesture charge as a very serious offence.

media:group.media:description: The incident happened outside the Observatory Bar on Hobart's waterfront.
